items.foco: Radial nerve: posterior branch of the brachial plexus (C5-C8)
items.title: Anatomical origin of the radial nerve
items.contentHtml:
Radial nerve: posterior branch of the brachial plexus (C5-C8)

  • Radial nerve is the continuation of the posterior branch of the brachial plexus with roots from C5 to C8 (occasionally T1).
  • Its course surrounds the humerus in the radial sulcus (canal of torsion), where it is vulnerable to compression.
  • Innervates the extensor muscles of the arm and forearm: triceps brachii, anconeus, brachioradialis, extensor carpi radialis longus and the posterior extensor compartment of the forearm.
  • The superficial sensory branch emerges in the distal forearm to innervate the dorsum of the hand.

items.foco: Mechanism: prolonged compression against the humerus
items.title: Injury mechanism
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism: prolonged compression against the humerus

  • Sustained compression of the radial nerve against the humeral shaft typically occurs when resting the arm on a hard surface for hours (chair bar, edge of table).
  • The classic context is Saturday night after alcohol ingestion or deep sedation that prevents postural changes.
  • Direct pressure causes focal neural ischemia and conduction block (neuropraxia) without axonal degeneration, which explains the good prognosis.
  • More severe injuries (axonotmesis) are rare and require high-energy trauma or very prolonged compression.

items.title: Cardinal clinical presentation
items.contentHtml:
Classic clinical presentation: wrist drop

  • Wrist drop is the inability to extend the wrist and fingers at the metacarpophalangeal level, with the wrist in passive flexion due to gravity.
  • The patient cannot perform active wrist extension nor extension of the fingers (except interphalangeal, lumbrical and interosseous, innervated by the ulnar and median nerve).
  • The elbow extension is usually preserved because the branch for the triceps emerges proximal to the radial groove.
  • The loss of thumb extension and thumb abduction (extensor brevis and longus, abductor longus) completes the picture.

items.foco: Sensory deficit: back of the hand (1st interosseous space)
items.title: Affected sensitive territory
items.contentHtml:
Sensory deficit: back of the hand (1st interosseous space)

  • The sensory loss is located on the dorsum of the hand, specifically in the 1st interosseous space (between thumb and index finger) and the dorsal surface of the thumb.
  • The exact territory corresponds to the superficial radial nerve (sensory branch) that innervates the radial dorsal skin of the hand to the proximal half of the index, middle and ring fingers (except distal phalanges).
  • It is important to differentiate between lesions of the median nerve (palmar aspect) and the ulnar nerve (dorsal and palmar ulnar border).
  • In Saturday palsy the sensory deficit is usually mild. or transient, with motor deficit predominating.

items.foco: Main affected muscle: common extensor of the fingers
items.title: Key muscle in radial paralysis
items.contentHtml:
Main affected muscle: common extensor of the fingers

  • The extensor commons of the fingers is the main extensor muscle of the fingers at the level of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints.
  • Its paralysis prevents the extension of the index, middle, ring and little fingers from the MCP, while interphalangeal extension (by lumbricals and interossei) is prevented. preserved.
  • Other muscles affected: extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis (extension and radial deviation of the wrist), extensor carpi ulnaris (extension and ulnar deviation), extensor of the index finger and extensor of the little finger.
  • The examination should include resisted extension of the wrist and extension of the fingers against resistance to adjust the force.

items.foco: Preserved movement: elbow extension (triceps sometimes intact)
items.title: Triceps preservation
items.contentHtml:
Preserved movement: elbow extension (triceps sometimes intact)

  • Elbow extension is preserved because the triceps brachii receives proximal innervation (branches of the radial nerve emerging before the radial sulcus).
  • In compressions distal to the sulcus (as in typical Sabbath palsy), triceps function is normal; if there is triceps weakness, it suggests a more proximal injury (e.g. brachial plexus or high humeral injury).
  • The anconeus (small extensor of the elbow) is also usually intact.
  • Intact elbow extension helps differentiate from an injury to the axillary nerve (shoulder abduction) or brachial plexus top.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis: axillary nerve or brachial plexus injury
items.title: Key differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis: injury to the axillary nerve or brachial plexus

  • Axillary nerve injury: shoulder abduction deficit (deltoid) and patch anesthesia over the shoulder (deltoid region), without involvement of wrist/finger extensors.
  • Brachial plexus injury (C5-C6): paralysis of Erb-Duchenne with loss of abduction, elbow flexion and external rotation, but preserved wrist extension.
  • Injury to the proximal (high) radial nerve: affects triceps and produces hand drop plus elbow extension weakness.
  • Injury to the posterior branch of the brachial plexus: affects the radial and axillary nerves, causing deficits in elbow extension and abduction of the shoulder next to the hand fall.

items.title: Electromyography in radial palsy
items.contentHtml:
Electromyography: confirms denervation in extensors

  • electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies confirm the diagnosis and evaluate the severity of the injury.
  • Typical findings: denervation (fibrillation potentials, positive waves) in extensor muscles of the forearm (extensor common of the fingers, extensor carpi radialis) with preservation of the triceps.
  • The motor conduction velocity of the radial nerve shows prolonged distal latency or conduction block in the compressed segment.
  • EMG is performed 2-3 weeks after onset to detect signs of denervation; helps differentiate neuropraxia (good prognosis) from axonotmesis (slower recovery).

items.title: Initial orthopedic management
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment: wrist extension splint

  • First-line treatment is the placement of a wrist extension splint (dynamic or static splint) that maintains the wrist in 20-30 degrees of extension to prevent flexion contractures and facilitate hand function.
  • A finger splint may be added to support the joints. metacarpophalangeals in extension if there is severe deformity.
  • Physiotherapy with passive range of motion exercises and progressive strengthening is essential to maintain joint mobility and avoid stiffness.
  • It is recommended to avoid repeated compression of the nerve and postural changes during sleep.

items.foco: Prognosis: favorable, recovery in weeks to months
items.title: Prognosis and expected evolution
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis: favorable, recovery in weeks to months

  • The prognosis is favorable in most cases because typical compression produces neuropraxia (reversible demyelinating injury) without significant axonal loss.
  • Motor recovery usually begins in 2 to 4 weeks and is completed in 3 to 6 months; sensory recovery may be more rapid.
  • Poor prognostic factors: no improvement at 3 months, complete denervation on EMG (axonotmesis), or traumatic injury with associated humeral fracture.
  • In cases of axonotmesis or neurotmesis (rare), recovery may take 6 to 12 months or require surgical repair.

items.foco: Typical cause: sleeping with a compressed arm (Saturday night)
items.title: Classic etiological context
items.contentHtml:
Typical cause: sleeping with a compressed arm (Saturday night)

  • The eponym Saturday paralysis (or Saturday night palsy) describes the compression of the radial nerve when sleeping with the arm hanging over the back of a chair or resting against a hard surface after ingestion of alcohol or sedatives.
  • alcoholic intoxication or drug use. Sedatives decrease the perception of pain and the ability to change position, prolonging compression.
  • Other causes: tourniquet compression, humerus fracture (especially the middle third), poorly adjusted crutches (crutcher's paralysis), or pressure during surgery with the arm in abduction.
  • The clinical history of having sleeping in an unusual position is key to the diagnosis.

items.foco: Avoid confusion: radial palsy vs. ulnar palsy
items.title: Differences with ulnar palsy
items.contentHtml:
Avoid confusion: radial palsy vs. ulnar palsy

  • Radial nerve palsy: produces wrist drop with inability to extend wrist and fingers, preserving elbow extension; sensory deficit on the back of the hand (1st interosseous space).
  • Ulnar nerve palsy: produces ulnar claw hand (MCF hyperextension and interphalangeal flexion of the ring and little finger), loss of adduction and abduction of fingers, and sensory deficit in the ulnar edge of the hand (5th finger and ulnar half of the 4th).
  • In ulnar palsy, extension of the wrist is preserved and the patient cannot cross the fingers (positive Froment's sign).
  • radial palsy does not affect the flexion of the fingers or the thumb-index clamp, functions of the median and ulnar.
